metadata.working_title: Clustered synapses develop in distinct dendritic domains in visual cortex before eye opening

abstract_sketch.problem: Synaptic inputs to adult cortical neurons are organized into clusters along dendrites, supporting high-fidelity dendritic computation, yet when and how this organization emerges during development is unresolved.
abstract_sketch.gap: Prior EM work established that synapses are added rapidly before eye opening, but no study has mapped the emergence of functional synaptic clustering at individual-synapse resolution in vivo during the developmental period that precedes sensory experience.
abstract_sketch.approach: We combined in vivo whole-cell voltage-clamp recordings with two-photon dendritic calcium imaging of GCaMP6s in layer 2/3 pyramidal neurons of mouse primary visual cortex, capturing 3440 synaptic transmission events at 354 individual synapses across 12 dendritic areas from 11 mice spanning P8 to P13.
abstract_sketch.key_results: The density of functional synapses and their transmission frequency rose steeply across the second postnatal week (spine vs shaft rate: 0.55 vs 0.16 min^-1 at P8-10; 0.67 vs 0.23 min^-1 at P12-13). High-activity synapses formed dendritic domains whose extent (18.7 to 22.5 um), synapse count (3.2 to 6.0), density (1.4 to 2.6 per 100 um) and dendritic coverage (27.2% to 61.3%) grew during the second postnatal week. Local co-activity was markedly higher inside domains (0.08 vs 0.02 at P8-10; 0.06 vs 0.04 at P12-13), and synapses with higher local co-activity showed positive Mann-Kendall plasticity scores, consistent with activity-driven stabilization.
abstract_sketch.impact: Functional dendritic domains assemble before eye opening, providing ready-made computational subunits that can support high-sensitivity processing the moment visual experience begins.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Functional brains require high-specificity wiring refined by activity-dependent mechanisms (Cline 2003; Martini 2021; Sanes-Zipursky 2020)
EM evidence: synapses emerge end of first postnatal week and peak addition before eye opening at P14 (Blue-Parnavelas 1983; DeFelipe 1997; Miller-Peters 1981; Wildenberg 2023)
Adult cortex shows clustering of functionally similar synapses (Iacaruso 2017; Wilson 2016; Kerlin 2019; Scholl 2021; Winnubst 2015; Takahashi 2012)
Clustered inputs support supralinear local dendritic integration (Branco-Hausser 2011; Harnett 2012; Losonczy-Magee 2006; Makara-Magee 2013; Larkum 2022)
Open questions: when do inputs become clustered, do synapses form in clusters or sort later, and do input patterns distribute smoothly or in distinct domains
Approach and contribution: in vivo whole-cell + two-photon mapping of V1 L2/3 neurons from P8 to P13, with an order-of-magnitude larger dendritic sampling than prior work

subsections.title: Mapping synaptic inputs onto spine and shaft synapses
content_bullets: Voltage-clamp + GCaMP6s + DsRed in L2/3 pyramidal cells; resonant + piezo imaging
354 functional synapses and 3440 transmission events across 12 dendrites from 11 P8-P13 mice
QX314 in pipette blocks APs; holding at -30 mV enables NMDA-dependent Ca2+ influx at both spine and shaft synapses

subsections.title: Synaptic inputs increase rapidly during the second postnatal week
content_bullets: Density of functional synapses rose significantly with age (linear regression; n=12 experiments)
Mean transmission frequency per synapse rose with age
Event count per dendrite higher at P12/13 (n=6) vs P8-10 (n=6)
Spine-to-shaft ratio unchanged with age
Rate distribution long-tailed: ~20% of synapses very active
Spine rates > shaft rates at both ages (P8-10: 0.55 +/- 0.09 vs 0.16 +/- 0.03 min^-1; P12-13: 0.67 +/- 0.07 vs 0.23 +/- 0.03 min^-1; p<1e-9 Mann-Whitney)
25 +/- 20% of spines inactive in young, 9 +/- 9% in older dendrites (29 +/- 8 and 26 +/- 6 min recordings)

subsections.title: Synaptic transmission aligns with somatic barrages
content_bullets: Barrages defined as inward currents >=10 pA
59% of events in barrages in young, 61% in older
Barrage rate unchanged with age
Per-barrage synaptic charge trends upward with age; events per barrage rise significantly
Events per barrage linearly correlate with total charge
Event amplitude higher during larger barrages suggesting multivesicular release

subsections.title: Synapses are spatially organized along developing dendrites
content_bullets: Inter-synapse distance distribution deviates from random shuffles in P8-P10 (KS test)
In P12-P13 synapse distribution is indistinguishable from random: dendrites become more uniformly covered
High-activity synapses defined as top 20th percentile per age
76% of high-activity synapses on spines
Synapses within 10 um of a high-activity synapse are more active than those farther away (n=181 <10 um vs 173 >10 um; t-test)
Relationship consistent across ages

subsections.title: Synaptic inputs are organized in functional domains
content_bullets: Domains: segments containing at least one high-activity synapse plus neighbors within 10 um
Synapses per domain 3.2 +/- 1.7 vs 6.0 +/- 3.2
Domain density 1.4 +/- 0.7 vs 2.6 +/- 1.0 per 100 um
Fraction of dendrite covered 27.2 +/- 16.2% vs 61.3 +/- 29.4%
Synapses within domains 46.2 +/- 7.4% (n=6) vs 72 +/- 23.2% (n=6); p=0.026
Local co-activity inside > outside: 0.08 +/- 0.02 vs 0.02 +/- 0.01 (young); 0.06 +/- 0.01 vs 0.04 +/- 0.01 (older); n=213 in, 173 out
Within-domain co-activity > between-domain co-activity (Wilcoxon signed-rank)

subsections.title: Local synaptic synchronicity correlates with synaptic activity changes
content_bullets: Mann-Kendall score (Hussain-Mahmud 2019) captures monotonic changes in per-synapse transmission frequency
Mann-Kendall score higher inside than outside domains (n=213 vs 173; t-test)
Per-synapse local co-activity (within 10 um proximal and distal) positively correlates with Mann-Kendall score (linear regression, synapses with rate >= 0.6 min^-1)
Model (Fig 7D): scattered synapses at P8 sort into co-active domains during second postnatal week

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Animals: 11 C57BL/6J pups P8-P13; IACUC approved (KNAW)
In utero electroporation at E16.5 of GCaMP6s (Addgene 40753; 2 mg/ml) and DsRed (pCAGGS; 0.5-2 mg/ml); 5 pulses at 30 V, 50 ms, 950 ms interval
In vivo whole-cell recording with Alexa 594-coated BSA pipettes (4.5-6 MOhm); 2% isoflurane for surgery, 0.7-1% maintenance
Intracellular solution: 120 mM CsMeSO3, 8 mM NaCl, 15 mM CsCl2, 10 mM TEA-Cl, 10 mM HEPES, 5 mM QX-314 bromide, 4 mM MgATP, 0.3 mM Na-GTP
Voltage-clamp at -30 mV; Multiclamp 700b; 10 kHz sampling, 3 kHz filter
Two-photon imaging on Nikon A1R-MP, 16x/0.8 NA water objective, Chameleon II Ti:sapphire; 5-15 Hz; 0.32 um pixel; z-spacing 1.5-2 um
Image processing: NoRMCorre drift correction; delta-F stacks from mean baseline; ROI drawing in ImageJ; event detection 2x noise threshold with manual review
Spine vs shaft classification from lateral appendages or 3D-reconstructed spines above/below shaft
Activity floor: transmission frequency >= 0.03 min^-1
Random shuffling test: empty positions at 4 +/- 1 um spacing; 100 shuffles; KS test
Co-activity: co-transmissions during shared barrages normalized by total events and comparison-group size
Domain definition: 10 um radius rules; 5 um padding at edges; 20 um split rule for synapses between two domains
Statistics: 2-sided Student's t-tests (paired and independent) for normal or low-n data; Mann-Whitney-U and Wilcoxon signed-rank for non-normal; linear regression for age; KS for distributions

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Key finding: functionally distinct dendritic domains assemble in V1 L2/3 dendrites before eye opening, and local co-activity correlates with synapse stabilization

Long-tailed per-synapse frequency reflects presynaptic release-probability heterogeneity (Markram 1997; Koester-Sakmann 2000) rather than firing-rate differences, since neonatal L2/3 firing is homogeneous (Rochefort 2009; Siegel 2012)
Synapses per domain grow from ~3 to ~6; consistent with connectomic evidence of multi-contact axons in mature cortex (Kasthuri 2015; Feldmeyer 2006; Winfield 1981)

Clustering features in adult vary by species (Iacaruso 2017; Wilson 2016); spontaneous retinal waves carry retinotopy, direction and orientation structure (Feller 1997; Meister 1991; Ackman 2012; Hanganu 2006; Kirchner-Gjorgjieva 2021) that could seed domains
Local co-activity drives clustering via BDNF/proBDNF push-pull plasticity (Winnubst 2015; Niculescu 2018) supporting a first-guess dendritic organization refined by experience after eye opening
Limitations: anesthesia reduces network-event frequency but preserves participation patterns (Siegel 2012); small dataset (n=11 mice); potential misclassification of z-plane spine synapses
